papers.title: The Prism Manifold Realization Problem
papers.why_relevant: Propositions 3.2 and 3.5 use diagonal-weight monotonicity and nonnegative-pairing decomposition arguments in positive-definite lattices. These methods are directly relevant to ruling out decompositions of vertices and isolating norm-one or rigid equality cases.

lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2
lemmas.statement: Let L=âˆ†(p,q) be a positive-definite D-type lattice with vertex basis x_*, x_**, x_0, â€¦, x_n. Thus L is the free abelian group on these generators with symmetric bilinear form determined by x_*Â·x_*=x_**Â·x_**=2, x_iÂ·x_i=a_i for 0â‰¤iâ‰¤n, x_*Â·x_0=x_**Â·x_0=âˆ’1, x_iÂ·x_j=âˆ’1 when |iâˆ’j|=1 and 0â‰¤i,jâ‰¤n, and all other pairings between distinct basis elements equal to 0; the integers a_i satisfy a_iâ‰¥2. An element â„“ of a positive-definite lattice is called irreducible if there do not exist nonzero lattice elements a,b with â„“=a+b and aÂ·bâ‰¥0. Then every vertex-basis element x_*, x_**, x_0, â€¦, x_n is irreducible in L.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a generator x_i and take any wâˆˆL with wâ‰ 0,x_i. Write w=w_*x_*+w_**x_**+Î£_{j=0}^n w_j x_j, and examine wÂ·(x_iâˆ’w). Treating the diagonal entries a_j as variables, the coefficient of a_i is w_i(1âˆ’w_i), and the coefficient of a_j for jâ‰ i is âˆ’w_j^2; since all w_j are integers, these coefficients are â‰¤0. Hence wÂ·(x_iâˆ’w) is non-increasing as any a_j is increased. It therefore suffices to prove negativity in the minimal case a_0=â€¦=a_n=2. In that case L is identified with the standard root lattice D_{n+3}={integer vectors in Z^{n+3} with even coordinate sum} by x_*â†¦e_{âˆ’2}+e_{âˆ’1}, x_**â†¦âˆ’e_{âˆ’2}+e_{âˆ’1}, and x_jâ†¦âˆ’e_{jâˆ’1}+e_j for jâ‰¥0. Each generator maps to a root Â±e_rÂ±e_s of norm 2. If such a root decomposed as Î±+Î² with Î±,Î² nonzero in D_{n+3} and Î±Â·Î²â‰¥0, then 2=|Î±+Î²|=|Î±|+|Î²|+2Î±Â·Î²â‰¥2+2, since every nonzero vector of D_{n+3} has norm at least 2. This is impossible. Thus wÂ·(x_iâˆ’w)<0 in the all-2 case, and by monotonicity also in the original lattice, proving irreducibility.

lemmas.label: Proposition 3.5
lemmas.statement: In the D-type lattice L=âˆ†(p,q) described above, for any subset A of the vertex-basis index set {*, **, 0, â€¦, n}, write [A]=Î£_{iâˆˆA} x_i. Let the pairing graph on A have vertex set A and an edge between two indices whenever the corresponding basis elements have nonzero pairing. An element xâˆˆL is called an interval if x=[A] for some A such that this pairing graph on A is connected and A does not contain both * and **. Then every interval is irreducible.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Let x=[A] be an interval and suppose w is a possible summand in a decomposition x=w+(xâˆ’w). Write x=Î£ c_i x_i with c_iâˆˆ{0,1}. In wÂ·(xâˆ’w), the coefficient of the diagonal variable a_i is w_i(c_iâˆ’w_i), which is â‰¤0 for every integer w_i because c_i is 0 or 1. Hence the same diagonal-weight monotonicity as in Proposition 3.2 reduces the irreducibility check to the case a_i=2 for all i. Under the standard embedding of this all-2 D-type lattice into D_{n+3}âŠ‚Z^{n+3}, a connected interval not using both fork vertices telescopes to a single root Â±e_rÂ±e_s: along the linear chain the intermediate basis vectors cancel, and including exactly one of x_* or x_** only changes the sign/choice of the left endpoint. Such a root has norm 2 and is irreducible in D_{n+3} by the same minimal-norm argument used in Proposition 3.2. Therefore no decomposition of [A] with nonnegative pairing exists in the all-2 case, and monotonicity gives the same conclusion for the original weights.

lemmas.label: Proposition 3.9
lemmas.statement: Let L=âˆ†(p,q) be a D-type lattice with vertex basis x_*, x_**, x_0, â€¦, x_n as above, and let an interval mean an element [A]=Î£_{iâˆˆA}x_i where A has connected pairing graph and does not contain both * and **. Let m be the smallest index with a_mâ‰¥3. For 0â‰¤jâ‰¤m define an involution Ï„_j on the vertex basis by Ï„_j(x_*)=x_**, Ï„_j(x_**)=x_*, Ï„_j(x_i)=x_i for 0â‰¤i<j, Ï„_j(x_j)=âˆ’x_*âˆ’x_**âˆ’2x_0âˆ’â‹¯âˆ’2x_{jâˆ’1}âˆ’x_j, and Ï„_j(x_i)=âˆ’x_i for i>j. Then for every nonzero irreducible element xâˆˆL, either x or âˆ’x is an interval, or there exists some 0â‰¤jâ‰¤m such that Ï„_j(x) or âˆ’Ï„_j(x) is an interval.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The maps Ï„_j are isometric involutions, so they preserve irreducibility. Write x=Î£ c_i x_i and replace x by âˆ’x if necessary so that c_0â‰¥0 and some coefficient is positive. First eliminate negative coefficients: if A={i:c_i<0} and y=Î£_{iâˆˆA}c_i x_i, then yÂ·(xâˆ’y) is a sum over edges crossing from negative to nonnegative coefficients, with terms âˆ’c_i c_kâ‰¥0. Irreducibility forces y=0, so all c_iâ‰¥0. The support B={i:c_iâ‰ 0} must have connected pairing graph, otherwise x splits as a sum of two orthogonal nonzero parts. If every c_i is 0 or 1, then x=[B]; this is an interval unless B contains both fork vertices, in which case connectedness gives B={*,**,0,â€¦,j} and âˆ’Ï„_0(x)=x_0+â‹¯+x_j is an interval. Now suppose some c_i>1. Let z=[B]. If 0âˆ‰B, then zÂ·(xâˆ’z)=Î£(a_iâˆ’2)(c_iâˆ’1)+endpoint terms is nonnegative, contradicting irreducibility; hence 0âˆˆB, and connectedness gives c_iâ‰¥1 for every 0â‰¤iâ‰¤max B. Let j be the largest index with c_j>1. If there were a largest k<j with c_k<c_j, take w=x_{k+1}+â‹¯+x_j; the formula wÂ·(xâˆ’w)=Î£_{k+1â‰¤iâ‰¤j}(a_iâˆ’2)(c_iâˆ’1)+(c_{k+1}âˆ’c_k)+(c_jâˆ’c_{j+1})âˆ’2 is nonnegative, again impossible. Thus c_iâ‰¥c_j>1 for all i<j. Finally test the two initial intervals w_0=x_0+â‹¯+x_j and w_1=x_*+x_**+w_0. Their pairings with xâˆ’w_r are respectively Î£_{0â‰¤iâ‰¤j}(a_iâˆ’2)(c_iâˆ’1) âˆ“(c_*+c_**âˆ’c_0)+(c_jâˆ’2)âˆ’c_{j+1}. Since one choice of sign makes the middle term nonnegative, irreducibility forces all nonnegative terms to vanish and c_{j+1}=1. Hence a_i=2 for iâ‰¤j, c_j=2, c_0=c_*+c_**, and j<m. Applying âˆ’Ï„_{j+1} kills the coefficient at x_j while leaving a nonzero coefficient at x_{j+1}; connected-support and 0/1-coefficient arguments then show âˆ’Ï„_{j+1}(x) is an interval.

papers.title: The Lens Space Realization Problem
papers.why_relevant: Proposition 3.3 gives a proof-level classification of irreducible elements in graph lattices as connected cuts. This is the main portable template if the given tree lattice can be compared to a graph lattice.

lemmas.label: Proposition 3.3
lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected loopless undirected multigraph, with e(u,v) denoting the number of edges between distinct vertices u and v and d(v) the degree of v. Let Î“Ì„(G) be the free abelian group on symbols [v], vâˆˆV, with symmetric bilinear form defined by âŸ¨[v],[v]âŸ©=d(v) and âŸ¨[u],[v]âŸ©=âˆ’e(u,v) for uâ‰ v. Its radical is generated by [V]=Î£_{vâˆˆV}[v], and the graph lattice is Î“(G)=Î“Ì„(G)/â„¤[V]. For TâŠ‚V, write [T]=Î£_{vâˆˆT}[v] in Î“(G). A nonzero element x of a lattice is irreducible if there do not exist nonzero y,z with x=y+z and âŸ¨y,zâŸ©â‰¥0. Then the irreducible elements of Î“(G) are exactly the elements Â±[T], where âˆ…â‰ TâŠŠV and both induced subgraphs G|T and G|(Vâˆ’T) are connected. In particular, for a single vertex u, the class [u] is irreducible exactly when Gâˆ’u is connected.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a root r and represent Î“(G) by the span of [v], vâ‰ r, so the coefficient at r is 0. Let 0â‰ x=Î£ c_v[v] be irreducible. Replacing x by âˆ’x if needed, let c=max c_vâ‰¥1 and T={v:c_v=c}; then râˆ‰T. Compute âŸ¨[T],xâˆ’[T]âŸ©=(câˆ’1)d(T)âˆ’Î£_{vâˆ‰T} c_v e(v,T)=Î£_{vâˆ‰T}(câˆ’1âˆ’c_v)e(v,T)â‰¥0. Since [T] is nonzero, irreducibility forces xâˆ’[T]=0. Thus every irreducible is Â±[T]. It remains to decide when [T] is irreducible. For any y=Î£ y_v[v], the key identity is âŸ¨y,yâˆ’[T]âŸ©=Î£_C Î£_{(u,v)âˆˆE(C)}(y_uâˆ’y_v)^2 + Î£_{(u,v)âˆˆE(T,Vâˆ’T)}(y_uâˆ’y_v)(y_uâˆ’y_vâˆ’1), where C ranges over the connected components of G|T and G|(Vâˆ’T), and the cut-edge term is oriented with uâˆˆT, vâˆˆVâˆ’T. All terms are nonnegative for integral y-values. If G|T is disconnected, take y to be the indicator of one component of G|T; then y and [T]âˆ’y are nonzero and orthogonal, so [T] is reducible. If G|(Vâˆ’T) is disconnected, take y=âˆ’[C] for a component C not containing the root; again [T]=([T]+[C])+(-[C]) is an orthogonal nontrivial decomposition. Conversely, if both sides are connected and [T]=y+z with âŸ¨y,zâŸ©â‰¥0, then âŸ¨y,yâˆ’[T]âŸ©=âˆ’âŸ¨y,zâŸ©â‰¤0, so the nonnegative identity forces equality throughout. Connectivity then makes y constant on T and on Vâˆ’T; since y_r=0 on the connected complement, y is 0 off T and is either 0 or 1 on T. Hence y=0 or y=[T], so no nontrivial reducing decomposition exists. Portable idea: when a weighted tree can be realized as such a graph lattice by adding a root and extra root-edges, irreducibility of original vertex classes becomes a connectedness-of-complement question.

lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4
lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected loopless undirected multigraph with no cut-edge. Fix a root râˆˆV and identify Î“(G) with the subgroup of Î“Ì„(G) spanned by [v] for vâ‰ r. Let TâŠ‚Vâˆ’{r}. Suppose [T]=y+z in Î“(G) and âŸ¨y,zâŸ©=âˆ’1. Then one of the following holds. (1) The induced subgraph G|T contains a cut-edge e; if deleting e splits G|T into vertex sets T1 and T2, then {y,z}={[T1],[T2]}. (2) The induced subgraph G|(Vâˆ’T) contains a cut-edge e; if deleting e splits G|(Vâˆ’T) into vertex sets T1 and T2 with râˆˆT2, then {y,z}={[T1âˆªT],âˆ’[T1]}.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Write z=[T]âˆ’y. Then âŸ¨y,zâŸ©=âˆ’1 is equivalent to âŸ¨y,yâˆ’[T]âŸ©=1. Use the same edge-sum identity as in Proposition 3.3: âŸ¨y,yâˆ’[T]âŸ© is the sum of squares (y_uâˆ’y_v)^2 over edges internal to components of G|T and G|(Vâˆ’T), plus terms (y_uâˆ’y_v)(y_uâˆ’y_vâˆ’1) over cut edges between T and Vâˆ’T. Each cut term is 0 or at least 2, and each square is nonnegative. Therefore the total value 1 must arise from a unique internal edge e on which the y-values differ by exactly 1; all other terms vanish. Hence e is a bridge in one of the induced subgraphs G|T or G|(Vâˆ’T), and y is constant on each component after e is removed. If e lies in G|T, let T1,T2 be the two vertex sets after deleting e. Because G itself has no cut-edge, each Ti has at least one edge to Vâˆ’T. Vanishing of all cut-edge terms forces the y-values on T1 and T2 to be 0 and 1 in some order, while y is 0 on the component of Vâˆ’T containing the root. Thus y=[T1] or [T2], and the decomposition is [T]=[T1]+[T2]. If e lies in G|(Vâˆ’T), let T2 be the component containing r and T1 the other component. Again, since e is not a global cut-edge, both T1 and T2 connect to T; the vanishing conditions force either y=âˆ’[T1] or y=[TâˆªT1]. This gives the second listed form. The no-cut-edge hypothesis is used exactly to force the existence of edges from both sides of the local bridge to the opposite side of the cut T,Vâˆ’T, which pins down the constants.

lemmas.label: Corollary 3.5
lemmas.statement: Let L=âŠ•_k L_k be an orthogonal direct sum of linear lattices. Here each L_k has an ordered basis x_1,â€¦,x_n with âŸ¨x_i,x_iâŸ©=a_iâ‰¥2, âŸ¨x_i,x_{i+1}âŸ©=âˆ’1, and all other off-diagonal pairings 0. In one summand, an interval means a nonempty consecutive set T={x_i,â€¦,x_j}, and [T]=Î£_{xâˆˆT}x. If two intervals T,Tâ€² in the same summand overlap but do not have the same left endpoint or the same right endpoint, write Tâ‹”Tâ€². A vector v is breakable if v=y+z with |y|,|z|â‰¥3 and âŸ¨y,zâŸ©=âˆ’1; otherwise it is unbreakable. Then: (1) the irreducible vectors in L are precisely the vectors Â±[T], where T is an interval in one summand L_k; (2) each summand L_k is indecomposable as an orthogonal direct sum of nonzero lattices; (3) if Tâ‹”Tâ€², then [Tâˆ’Tâ€²]+[Tâ€²âˆ’T] and [Tâˆ’Tâ€²]âˆ’[Tâ€²âˆ’T] are reducible, interpreting [A] as the sum over the basis elements in A; (4) [T] is unbreakable if and only if T contains at most one basis vertex x_i with a_iâ‰¥3, equivalently at most one vertex of degree at least 3 in the associated graph model.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Model a linear lattice as a graph lattice: take a root r, a chain of vertices x_1,â€¦,x_n, edges x_i x_{i+1}, and enough parallel edges from r to x_i so that the degree of x_i is a_i. An orthogonal sum of linear lattices is obtained by wedging the corresponding chains at the same root. Applying Proposition 3.3, a connected cut T not containing the root whose complement is connected must lie entirely in one chain and must be a consecutive interval; this proves the classification of irreducibles. For indecomposability, the vertex basis elements x_i are irreducible and their pairing graph is connected within each chain. In any orthogonal decomposition, an irreducible vector lies wholly in one summand; connectivity forces all x_i in a chain into the same summand, so L_k is indecomposable. For (3), if overlapping intervals T,Tâ€² do not share an endpoint, their symmetric difference is a union of two separated pieces in the chain (or one interval split into two separated pieces when one interval lies strictly inside the other). The corresponding two nonzero sums are orthogonal, so either sign choice gives a nontrivial decomposition with inner product 0, hence a reducible vector. For (4), if T contains two vertices of degree at least 3, split T at an edge between them into consecutive intervals T1,T2, each with norm at least 3; then [T]=[T1]+[T2] and âŸ¨[T1],[T2]âŸ©=âˆ’1, so [T] is breakable. Conversely, if [T]=y+z is a breaking with âŸ¨y,zâŸ©=âˆ’1, Proposition 3.4 applies to the associated graph, which has no cut-edge. The alternative in which the bridge lies in the complement of T gives one side of norm 2, so it cannot be a breaking with both norms at least 3. Thus the bridge lies inside T, splitting it into two intervals both of norm at least 3; each such interval must contain a degree â‰¥3 vertex. Hence T contains at least two high-degree vertices. This proves the equivalence.

papers.title: Bounds on Alternating Surgery Slopes
papers.why_relevant: Proposition 3.4 turns an obtuse superbase into a graph lattice, and Lemma 3.5 gives mutation techniques for superbases. This directly matches the near-obtuse superbase formulation with one acute pair.

lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected undirected graph with no self-loops, allowing multiple edges. Let Î›(G) be the graph lattice: start with the free abelian group on V, impose the relation [V]=Î£_{vâˆˆV}v=0, and use the bilinear form induced by uÂ·u=d(u) and uÂ·v=-e(u,v) for uâ‰ v, where d(u) is the valence of u and e(u,v) is the number of edges between u and v. For RâŠ†V write [R] for the class of Î£_{vâˆˆR}v in Î›(G). A nonzero vector z in a positive-definite lattice is called irreducible if there do not exist nonzero vectors x,y with z=x+y and xÂ·yâ‰¥0. Then a nonzero vector zâˆˆÎ›(G) is irreducible if and only if z=[R] for some nonempty proper subset RâŠ‚V such that both induced subgraphs G[R] and G[V\R] are connected.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the Laplacian/energy model of the graph lattice: an element is an integer-valued function on V modulo constants, and the pairing is Î£_{edges ab}(f(a)-f(b))(g(a)-g(b)). For necessity, represent z by an integer function f with min f=0. If max f=mâ‰¥2, let R_i={v: f(v)â‰¥i}. Then z=Î£_{i=1}^m [R_i]. Since R_jâŠ†R_i for i<j, one has [R_i]Â·[R_j]=e(R_j,V\R_i)â‰¥0; hence z=[R_1]+Î£_{iâ‰¥2}[R_i] is a nontrivial decomposition with nonnegative pairing, contradicting irreducibility. Thus max f=1 and z=[R]. If G[R] is disconnected, split R=AâŠ”B with no edges between A and B; then [R]=[A]+[B] and [A]Â·[B]=0. If G[V\R] is disconnected, use [R]=-[V\R] and the same argument on the complement. Hence both sides must be connected. Conversely, suppose R and V\R are connected and [R]=a+b. Choose integer functions f,g with f+g=1_R modulo constants. For each edge, orient so Î´(1_R) is 0 or 1. The contribution to aÂ·b is Î´fÂ·Î´g. If the edge stays on one side of the cut, Î´g=-Î´f and the contribution is -(Î´f)^2â‰¤0. If the edge crosses the cut, Î´g=1-Î´f and the contribution is Î´f(1-Î´f)â‰¤0 because Î´f is integral. Thus aÂ·bâ‰¤0. If aÂ·bâ‰¥0, every edge contribution is 0. Then f is constant on G[R] and on G[V\R], and across each cut edge the constant difference is 0 or 1. Since both sides are connected, f represents either 0 or [R] modulo constants, so one of a,b is zero. This proves irreducibility.

lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4
lemmas.statement: Let L be a positive-definite integral lattice of rank r. An obtuse superbase for L is a set B={v_0,...,v_r}âŠ‚L such that v_1,...,v_r form a Z-basis of L, v_0+...+v_r=0, and v_iÂ·v_jâ‰¤0 for every iâ‰ j. Given such B, define a finite undirected graph G_B with vertex set B and exactly |v_iÂ·v_j|=-v_iÂ·v_j edges between v_i and v_j for iâ‰ j. Then G_B is connected, and L is isomorphic, as an integral lattice, to the graph lattice Î›(G_B).
lemmas.proof_sketch: First prove connectedness. If R is the vertex set of a nonempty connected component of G_B, let s_R=Î£_{xâˆˆR}xâˆˆL. For any yâˆˆB outside R there are no edges from y to R, so yÂ·s_R=0. For yâˆˆR, use Î£_{xâˆˆB}x=0 and the absence of edges from R to B\R to get yÂ·s_R=-yÂ·Î£_{xâˆ‰R}x=0. Thus s_R pairs trivially with every element of B. Since B spans L and L is positive definite, s_R=0. But because v_1,...,v_r are a basis and v_0=-Î£_{i=1}^r v_i, the only linear relation among the r+1 elements of B is an integral multiple of Î£_{xâˆˆB}x=0. Hence no proper nonempty subcollection can sum to zero, so R=B and G_B is connected. Now define a homomorphism from the free abelian group on the vertices of G_B to L by sending each graph vertex to the corresponding vector in B. The total vertex sum maps to 0, so it descends to Î›(G_B). It is surjective because v_1,...,v_r are a basis, and the ranks agree because G_B is connected. The pairing is preserved: for distinct vertices it is preserved by the definition of edge multiplicity, and for a vertex v_k the graph valence is Î£_{iâ‰ k}(-v_kÂ·v_i)=v_kÂ·v_k, using v_0+...+v_r=0. Hence the descended map is an isomorphism of lattices.

lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5
lemmas.statement: Let L be an indecomposable positive-definite integral lattice, meaning L is not an orthogonal direct sum of two nonzero sublattices, and suppose L has an obtuse superbase B. Let vâˆˆB and suppose v=x+y for some x,yâˆˆL with xÂ·y=-1. Then there are unique vertices u_1,u_2âˆˆB\{v} such that u_1Â·x>0 and u_2Â·y>0, respectively, and the set B'=(B\{v,u_1,u_2})âˆª{x,y,u_1+u_2} is also an obtuse superbase for L.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Proposition 3.4 to identify L with the graph lattice of the graph G_B associated to the obtuse superbase B. The paper uses Lemma 3.2, which says that indecomposability of L is equivalent to G_B being 2-connected. Apply Lemma 3.3 to the 2-connected graph G_B, the vertex v, and the decomposition v=x+y with xÂ·y=-1. That lemma gives a cut edge e in G_B\{v}; after removing e, the graph G_B\{v} splits into two connected sides R and S, with endpoints u_1âˆˆR and u_2âˆˆS. Up to interchanging x and y, one has x=v+[R] and y=v+[S] in the graph lattice. The endpoints u_1,u_2 are characterized by xÂ·u_1=1 and yÂ·u_2=1, and no other vertex distinct from v,u_1,u_2 pairs positively with x or y; this gives the required uniqueness. The new set B' has total sum zero because x+y=v, so replacing v,u_1,u_2 by x,y,u_1+u_2 preserves the superbase relation. The cut-edge description also gives the needed unimodular change of spanning set: RâˆªS=B\{v}, and the replacement is the standard graph-lattice move corresponding to splitting along the cut edge and replacing its endpoints by their sum. Finally check obtuseness. Existing pairings among vertices not removed stay nonpositive. Lemma 3.3 gives wÂ·xâ‰¤0 and wÂ·yâ‰¤0 for every old vertex w outside {v,u_1,u_2}. Also xÂ·y=-1. Since B was obtuse, (u_1+u_2) pairs nonpositively with all remaining old vertices. The only delicate pairings are with x and y: the unique cut edge between u_1 and u_2 contributes a negative term that cancels the unique positive endpoint pairing, giving (u_1+u_2)Â·xâ‰¤0 and (u_1+u_2)Â·yâ‰¤0. Hence all distinct elements of B' have nonpositive pairing, and B' is an obtuse superbase.

papers.title: Lattice cohomology of normal surface singularities
papers.why_relevant: Contains the inverse-matrix/effective-versus-nef cone sign arguments that translate to positivity of inverses for branch M-matrices. These techniques are well suited to Schur-complement estimates and equality-case analysis.

lemmas.label: Â§2.2.3 (Positive cones and sign of the dual basis)
lemmas.statement: Let Î“ be a connected negative definite plumbing or resolution graph, and let L=âŠ•_{jâˆˆJ} Z E_j be its intersection lattice, with symmetric form ( , ). Thus the intersection matrix I=((E_i,E_j)) is negative definite, has negative diagonal entries, and has non-negative off-diagonal entries given by incidences of the graph. Put L_Q=LâŠ—Q. For each j, let D_jâˆˆL_Q be the dual element characterized by (D_j,E_i)=Î´_{ji} for every i. Define the effective cone L_Q,e={Î£_j r_j E_j : r_jâ‰¥0 for all j} and the numerically effective cone L_Q,ne={xâˆˆL_Q : (x,E_j)â‰¥0 for all j}. Then L_Q,ne is exactly the positive cone generated by the D_j: L_Q,ne={Î£_j r_j D_j : r_jâ‰¥0 for all j}. Moreover, because I is negative definite and Î“ is connected, every coefficient of every D_j in the basis {E_i} is strictly negative. Consequently âˆ’L_Q,neâŠ‚L_Q,e; in fact, every nonzero element of L_Q,ne has strictly negative E_i-coefficients.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The equality L_Q,ne={Î£ r_j D_j} is just dual-basis linear algebra: if x=Î£ r_j D_j then (x,E_i)=r_i, and conversely r_i=(x,E_i). The sign assertion is the key maximum-principle/M-matrix fact. Write A=âˆ’I. Then A is symmetric positive definite, has positive diagonal entries, non-positive off-diagonal entries, and is irreducible because Î“ is connected. Hence A is a nonsingular irreducible M-matrix, so A^{-1} has strictly positive entries. Equivalently, solve Ax=e_j. The solution minimizes 1/2 z^T A zâˆ’e_j^T z; replacing z by its positive part cannot increase this functional when off-diagonal entries are non-positive, so xâ‰¥0. If some coordinate x_i were 0, the equation (Ax)_i=0 for iâ‰ j forces all adjacent coordinates also 0; connectedness propagates this to x_j=0, contradicting (Ax)_j=1. Thus x>0. Since D_j=I^{-1}e_j=âˆ’A^{-1}e_j, all coefficients of D_j are strictly negative. The inclusion âˆ’L_Q,neâŠ‚L_Q,e follows by taking non-negative combinations. In the original positive-definite-tree setting, this is the same statement after negating the form on a branch: inverse matrices of positive definite Stieltjes branch matrices have positive entries.

lemmas.label: Â§2.2.4 (Canonical liftings to the effective and nef cones)
lemmas.statement: Keep the notation of Â§2.2.3, and let Lâ€² be the dual lattice embedded in L_Q. Let H=Lâ€²/L. Use the coefficientwise partial order on L_Q: xâ‰¤y means yâˆ’xâˆˆL_Q,e. For each class h=lâ€²+LâˆˆH there is a unique minimal effective representative lâ€²_e(h)âˆˆ(lâ€²+L)âˆ©L_Q,e. The set of all these representatives is Q={Î£_j r_j E_jâˆˆLâ€² : 0â‰¤r_j<1 for every j}. Similarly, for each h=lâ€²+LâˆˆH, the intersection (lâ€²+L)âˆ©L_Q,ne has a unique maximal element, denoted lâ€²_ne(h), and the intersection (lâ€²+L)âˆ©(âˆ’L_Q,ne) has a unique minimal element, denoted overline{lâ€²}_ne(h). These two nef liftings satisfy overline{lâ€²}_ne(h)=âˆ’lâ€²_ne(âˆ’h).
lemmas.proof_sketch: For the effective representative, write any representative in E-coordinates and reduce each coordinate modulo Z into the interval [0,1). This gives an element of Q in the same L-coset. Any other effective representative has the same fractional parts and differs from it by an integral cycle with all coefficients â‰¥0, so the element in Q is the unique coefficientwise minimum. For the nef representative, non-emptiness follows from the existence of an effective integral Z with (Z,E_j)<0 for all j: for mâ‰«0, lâ€²âˆ’mZ has all pairings with E_j positive. Such a Z is obtained from Â§2.2.3 by taking a positive integral multiple of âˆ’Î£_j D_j. Every nef element is anti-effective by âˆ’L_Q,neâŠ‚L_Q,e, hence all elements of a fixed nef coset are coefficientwise bounded above. The crucial closure property is: if x,y are nef representatives in the same coset, then z=max(x,y) coefficientwise is also nef. Indeed write z=x+a=y+b with a,b effective and with disjoint supports. For any vertex E_j, at least one of a_j,b_j is 0. If a_j=0, then (a,E_j)â‰¥0 because a is effective and has no E_j term, while off-diagonal intersections are non-negative; hence (z,E_j)=(x,E_j)+(a,E_j)â‰¥0. The case b_j=0 is identical. Thus z is nef. Since the coset is discrete and bounded above, choose a nef representative maximizing the sum of its coefficients; closure under max then shows it dominates every other nef representative, giving the unique maximum lâ€²_ne(h). Applying this to âˆ’h and negating gives the unique minimal element in the negative nef cone and the identity overline{lâ€²}_ne(h)=âˆ’lâ€²_ne(âˆ’h).

lemmas.label: Proposition 6.1.4 (Minimal nef reduction and generalized Laufer algorithm)
lemmas.statement: Let Ï€:(X_tilde,E)â†’(X,0) be a good resolution of a normal surface singularity whose exceptional intersection form is negative definite. Let L=âŠ•_{jâˆˆJ} Z E_j, let Lâ€²âŠ‚L_Q be the dual lattice, let L_e be the semigroup of effective integral cycles, and let L_Q,ne={xâˆˆL_Q : (x,E_j)â‰¥0 for all j}. For any lâ€²âˆˆLâ€² there exists a unique minimal element l_{lâ€²}âˆˆL_e such that e(lâ€²):=lâ€²âˆ’l_{lâ€²} lies in L_Q,ne. This cycle is obtained by the following algorithm. Start with x_0=0. If lâ€²âˆ’x_iâˆˆL_Q,ne, stop and set t=i. Otherwise choose any index j(i) with (lâ€²âˆ’x_i,E_{j(i)})<0 and define x_{i+1}=x_i+E_{j(i)}. The algorithm stops after finitely many steps and its final value is x_t=l_{lâ€²}, independently of the choices made. Moreover, if mathcal L is a holomorphic line bundle on X_tilde with c_1(mathcal L)=lâ€², and K is the canonical cycle, with Ï‡(l)=âˆ’(l,l+K)/2 for lâˆˆL, then h^1(mathcal L)=h^1(mathcal LâŠ—O_{X_tilde}(âˆ’l_{lâ€²}))âˆ’(lâ€²,l_{lâ€²})âˆ’Ï‡(l_{lâ€²}). In particular, the computation of h^1 for an arbitrary Chern class reduces, up to the displayed explicit lattice term, to the case of a line bundle whose Chern class is nef.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence: choose an effective integral Z with (Z,E_j)<0 for every j, possible by the inverse-sign result of Â§2.2.3. Then for mâ‰«0, lâ€²âˆ’mZ is nef, so at least one effective l with lâ€²âˆ’l nef exists. If l_1,l_2 are two such effective cycles, let l=min(l_1,l_2) coefficientwise and write x_i=l_iâˆ’l. The supports of x_1 and x_2 are disjoint. For a fixed E_j, choose i with coefficient of E_j in x_i equal to 0; then (x_i,E_j)â‰¥0 because x_i is effective and has no E_j term. Hence (lâ€²âˆ’l,E_j)=(lâ€²âˆ’l_i,E_j)+(x_i,E_j)â‰¥0. Thus the coefficientwise minimum of two admissible cycles is admissible, giving a unique minimal admissible cycle l_{lâ€²}. Algorithm: prove inductively that every x_i constructed satisfies x_iâ‰¤l_{lâ€²}. If x_iâ‰¤l_{lâ€²} but x_i+E_jâ‰¤l_{lâ€²} failed for a chosen j, then E_j has coefficient 0 in l_{lâ€²}âˆ’x_i, so (l_{lâ€²}âˆ’x_i,E_j)â‰¥0. Since lâ€²âˆ’l_{lâ€²} is nef, this gives (lâ€²âˆ’x_i,E_j)â‰¥0, contradicting the rule for choosing j. Therefore the algorithm is bounded above by l_{lâ€²}; since one coefficient increases by 1 at each step, it terminates. Its terminal x_t is admissible, hence by minimality equals l_{lâ€²}. For the cohomological formula, along the algorithm use exact sequences 0â†’mathcal LâŠ—O(âˆ’x_{i+1})â†’mathcal LâŠ—O(âˆ’x_i)â†’mathcal LâŠ—O_{E_{j(i)}}(âˆ’x_i)â†’0. The chosen inequality says deg(mathcal LâŠ—O_{E_{j(i)}}(âˆ’x_i))=(lâ€²âˆ’x_i,E_{j(i)})<0, so H^0 of the curve term vanishes. Therefore the change in h^1 is minus the Euler characteristic of that curve term, equal to âˆ’(lâ€²,x_{i+1}âˆ’x_i)+Ï‡(x_i)âˆ’Ï‡(x_{i+1}). Summing over i telescopes to the stated formula.

papers.title: On the OzsvÃ¡thâ€“SzabÃ³ invariant of negative definite plumbed 3â€“manifolds
papers.why_relevant: After reversing signs, the hypothesis becomes a negative-definite plumbing tree with one bad vertex. NÃ©methi's minimal anti-nef cycles and generalized Laufer algorithm provide portable sign-control and induction tools on rooted branches.

lemmas.label: Lemma 5.4 and Proposition 5.7
lemmas.statement: Let Î“ be a connected negative definite plumbing graph with vertex set J and lattice L=âŠ•_{jâˆˆJ} Z b_j, where (b_j,b_j)=e_j and (b_i,b_j)=1 if i and j are adjacent and 0 otherwise. Embed L'=Hom_Z(L,Z) in L_Q=LâŠ—Q using the form, and order L_Q coefficientwise in the basis {b_j}. Put S_Q={yâˆˆL_Q : (y,b_j)â‰¤0 for every jâˆˆJ}. Let KâˆˆL' be the canonical characteristic element, K(b_j)=âˆ’e_jâˆ’2. For any characteristic orbit [k]=K+2(l'+L), the intersection (l'+L)âˆ©S_Q has a unique minimal element, denoted l'_[k]. If k_r=K+2l'_[k], then: (a) (l'_[k],b_j)â‰¥b_j^2+1 for every jâˆˆJ; (b) k_r(x)â‰¥x^2 for every effective integral cycle xâˆˆL, xâ‰¥0; and (c) Ï‡_{k_r}(âˆ’x)â‰¥0 for every effective xâˆˆL, where Ï‡_{k_r}(y)=âˆ’(k_r(y)+y^2)/2.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Nonemptiness of (l'+L)âˆ©S_Q comes by adding to l' a sufficiently large integral effective cycle with all pairings strictly negative; negative definiteness of the plumbing matrix gives such cycles. For uniqueness/minimality, if l'+l_1 and l'+l_2 are in S_Q, set l=min(l_1,l_2) coefficientwise. For each basis vector b_j, one of l_1âˆ’l or l_2âˆ’l has zero b_j-coefficient; because off-diagonal intersections are nonnegative, subtracting that effective cycle cannot increase the pairing with b_j. Hence l'+l remains in S_Q. Closure under coefficientwise minimum gives a unique minimal element. For (a), if (l'_[k],b_j)â‰¤b_j^2 for some j, then l'_[k]âˆ’b_j is still in S_Q: at b_j this is exactly the assumed inequality, at adjacent vertices the pairing drops by 1, and elsewhere it is unchanged. This contradicts minimality. For (b), first (a) implies k_r(b_j)=2(l'_[k],b_j)âˆ’b_j^2âˆ’2â‰¥b_j^2 for every vertex. Suppose a nonzero effective x is a minimal counterexample to k_r(x)â‰¥x^2. For every b_j in the support of x, minimality gives k_r(xâˆ’b_j)â‰¥(xâˆ’b_j)^2, while k_r(x)<x^2. Combining these inequalities yields (l'_[k]âˆ’x,b_j)â‰¤0 for all b_j in the support of x. For vertices outside the support, (l'_[k]âˆ’x,b_j)â‰¤(l'_[k],b_j)â‰¤0 because x is effective and off-diagonal intersections are nonnegative. Thus l'_[k]âˆ’xâˆˆ(l'_[k]+L)âˆ©S_Q, contradicting minimality of l'_[k]. Finally, (c) is the identity 2Ï‡_{k_r}(âˆ’x)=k_r(x)âˆ’x^2 together with (b).

lemmas.label: Lemmas 7.3â€“7.4
lemmas.statement: In the same negative definite plumbing-lattice setup, fix a characteristic orbit [k]=K+2(l'+L), let l_min=l'_[k] be the unique minimal element of (l'+L)âˆ©S_Q, and put k_r=K+2l_min. Define S_[k]={xâˆˆL : (x+l_min,b_j)â‰¤0 for every jâˆˆJ}. For every xâˆˆL there is a unique minimal element y(x)âˆˆL such that xâ‰¤y(x) and y(x)âˆˆS_[k]. Moreover y(x) is obtained by the following generalized Laufer algorithm: set x_0=x; if x_lâˆ‰S_[k], choose any vertex j with (x_l+l_min,b_j)>0 and set x_{l+1}=x_l+b_j; stop when x_lâˆˆS_[k]. The algorithm terminates, its terminal value is y(x), and at every step Ï‡_{k_r}(x_{l+1})â‰¤Ï‡_{k_r}(x_l). More precisely, for a step adding b_j one has Ï‡_{k_r}(x_l+b_j)âˆ’Ï‡_{k_r}(x_l)=1âˆ’(x_l+l_min,b_j).
lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence and uniqueness of y(x) are proved exactly as for the minimal anti-nef representative: the set of elements of S_[k] above x is nonempty by adding a large anti-nef effective cycle, and it is closed under coefficientwise minimum. For the algorithm, let y=y(x). The key induction is that every intermediate x_l satisfies x_lâ‰¤y. If x_lâ‰¤y and the algorithm chooses j with (x_l+l_min,b_j)>0, then the b_j-coefficient of x_l must be strictly smaller than that of y. Otherwise yâˆ’x_l is effective with zero b_j-coefficient, so (x_l+l_min,b_j)=(y+l_min,b_j)âˆ’(yâˆ’x_l,b_j)â‰¤0, since yâˆˆS_[k] and (yâˆ’x_l,b_j)â‰¥0; contradiction. Hence adding b_j keeps x_{l+1}â‰¤y. Since coefficients are bounded above by those of y and increase at each step, the process terminates. The terminal point lies in S_[k] and is â‰¤y, so by minimality it equals y. The monotonicity of Ï‡ is the displayed difference formula: the chosen pairing is a positive integer, hence 1âˆ’(x_l+l_min,b_j)â‰¤0. This is the portable sign-control feature: adding a vertex with positive anti-nef defect never raises Ï‡.

lemmas.label: Lemmas 7.6â€“7.7 and Lemma 9.1
lemmas.statement: Let Î“ be a connected negative definite plumbing tree with a distinguished vertex 0, and write J*=Jâˆ–{0}. Assume Î“ is almost-rational with distinguished vertex 0, meaning that replacing e_0 by some e'_0â‰¤e_0 gives a rational graph, i.e. a graph whose canonical function Ï‡_K satisfies Ï‡_K(z)â‰¥1 for every nonzero effective integral cycle z. Fix a characteristic orbit [k], let l=l'_[k] be the minimal anti-nef representative, and set k=K+2l. For each integer iâ‰¥0, there is a unique minimal cycle x(i)âˆˆL satisfying m_0(x(i))=i and (x(i)+l,b_j)â‰¤0 for every jâˆˆJ*, where m_0 denotes the b_0-coefficient; moreover x(i)â‰¥0. The rooted Laufer algorithm from x(i) to x(i+1) is: start with x_0=x(i), x_1=x(i)+b_0; for lâ‰¥1, if some jâˆˆJ* satisfies (x_l+l,b_j)>0, choose such a j=j(l) and set x_{l+1}=x_l+b_j; stop when all pairings with vertices in J* are â‰¤0. This terminates at x(i+1), and for l>0 one has Ï‡_k(x_{l+1})â‰¤Ï‡_k(x_l), while Ï‡_k(x(i)+b_0)âˆ’Ï‡_k(x(i))=1âˆ’(x(i)+l,b_0); in particular the first step raises Ï‡_k exactly when x(i)+l is anti-nef also at vertex 0. Under the almost-rational hypothesis, the following stronger conclusions hold for every iâ‰¥0: (a) if xâˆˆL has m_0(x)=i, then Ï‡_k(x)â‰¥Ï‡_k(x(i)); (b) if y>x(i) and m_0(y)=i, then for some vertex b_j in the support of yâˆ’x(i), with jâˆˆJ*, one has Ï‡_k(yâˆ’b_j)â‰¤Ï‡_k(y); (c) in any rooted Laufer sequence from x(i) to x(i+1), every step after the initial b_0-step has exactly (x_l+l,b_{j(l)})=1 and hence Ï‡_k(x_{l+1})=Ï‡_k(x_l) for 0<l<t. Consequently Ï‡_k(x(i)+b_0)=Ï‡_k(x(i+1)).
lemmas.proof_sketch: For existence of x(i), one first constructs some cycle with b_0-coefficient i and nonpositive pairings along J* by taking a large anti-nef cycle and subtracting a suitable multiple of b_0; uniqueness follows because the set of such cycles is closed under coefficientwise minimum. Effectivity uses minimality of l: if x(i)=x_1âˆ’x_2 with disjoint effective positive and negative parts, then lâˆ’x_2 remains in S_Q, forcing x_2=0. The rooted algorithm is the same bounded-above induction as Lemma 7.4, now only adding vertices in J*: x(i+1) is an upper bound, and a chosen positive pairing forces the chosen coefficient to be below that in x(i+1). The Ï‡ identities again come from Ï‡_k(x+b_j)âˆ’Ï‡_k(x)=1âˆ’(x+l,b_j). For the AR refinements, use that any cycle supported away from the distinguished vertex lies in a rational subgraph of the rational graph obtained by decreasing e_0; hence its canonical Ï‡ is positive when nonzero. For (a), write any x with m_0(x)=i as x(i)âˆ’y_1+y_2 with y_1,y_2â‰¥0, disjoint support, and no b_0 in either support. Then Ï‡_k(x)=Ï‡_k(x(i)âˆ’y_1)+Ï‡_K(y_2)+(y_1,y_2)âˆ’(x(i)+l,y_2)â‰¥Ï‡_k(x(i)âˆ’y_1), using rationality for Ï‡_K(y_2), disjointness for (y_1,y_2)â‰¥0, and anti-nefness of x(i)+l on J*. Lemma 7.8 then gives Ï‡_k(x(i)âˆ’y_1)â‰¥Ï‡_k(x(i)). For (b), if every removal yâˆ’b_j raised Ï‡_k, then for all b_j in supp(yâˆ’x(i)) one gets (y+l,b_j)â‰¥b_j^2+2. Since (x(i)+l,b_j)â‰¤0, this implies (yâˆ’x(i)+K,b_j)â‰¥0 on the support; summing over coefficients gives Ï‡_K(yâˆ’x(i))â‰¤0, contradicting rationality of the relevant subgraph. For (c), set z_l=x_lâˆ’x(i). For l>0, the chosen inequality and anti-nefness of x(i)+l imply (z_l,b_{j(l)})>0. In the rational graph obtained by decreasing e_0, these same positive intersections occur, and the sequence can be completed to Laufer's computation of the fundamental cycle. Rationality forces all positive intersections in such a computation sequence to be exactly 1; hence (z_l,b_{j(l)})=1, and because (x_l+l,b_{j(l)}) is a positive integer differing from this by the nonpositive number (x(i)+l,b_{j(l)}), it equals 1. Thus Ï‡_k is constant after the initial step. A graph with all non-distinguished vertices satisfying âˆ’e_jâ‰¥Î´_j is AR by decreasing e_0 enough, which is why this lemma is directly portable to one-bad-vertex plumbing trees after sign reversal.
